intent: Check whether the candidate understands Dependency Injection as inversion of object creation and can explain why NestJS uses it to manage providers, module boundaries, scopes, and testability.
primaryQuestion: What is Dependency Injection, and why does NestJS use it?
expectedSignals: Defines Dependency Injection as receiving dependencies from an external container instead of constructing them inside the class.
Connects DI to inversion of control: the NestJS container owns provider creation, dependency resolution, and lifecycle management.
Explains why this improves testability because dependencies can be mocked or overridden by token.
Mentions maintainability benefits such as loose coupling, module visibility rules, reusable providers, and easier swapping of implementations.
Uses a concrete example such as UsersController receiving UsersService through the constructor instead of calling new UsersService().
redFlags: Describes DI only as importing a class from another file.
Says DI is just a NestJS decorator feature without explaining object ownership or lifecycle.
Cannot explain why manually creating service instances makes testing and configuration harder.
Ignores module registration and assumes every class is injectable automatically.

scoringHints.description: Clearly explains DI, IoC, provider tokens, lifecycle ownership, module registration, and testability with a NestJS example.
Explains constructor-provided dependencies and testability but misses lifecycle, module visibility, or token resolution details.
Knows that NestJS injects services but cannot explain why the pattern matters in real code.
Answer is generic framework language and does not demonstrate understanding of DI mechanics.

followUps.question: Can you show the difference between a controller using constructor injection and a controller calling new UsersService() directly?
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete comparison between DI and manual instantiation.

followUps.question: What would become harder to test or configure if the service created its own dependencies?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands the practical value of inversion of control.

en.title: Dependency Injection purpose in NestJS
en.displayQuestion: What is Dependency Injection, and why does NestJS use it?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands DI as container-managed object wiring, not just a decorator convention.
guidance: Define the pattern in terms of who creates and supplies dependencies.
Connect the explanation to NestJS providers, modules, lifecycle, and testing.
Use a small controller-service example to make the answer concrete.
commonMistakes: Treating DI as the same thing as importing a class.
Skipping why the IoC container makes mocking and replacement easier.
Ignoring that providers still need to be registered in a module context.

vi.title: Mục đích của Dependency Injection trong NestJS
vi.displayQuestion: Dependency Injection là gì, và vì sao NestJS dùng nó?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có hiểu DI là cơ chế container quản lý việc tạo và nối dependency, không chỉ là cú pháp decorator.
guidance: Định nghĩa pattern theo hướng ai tạo dependency và ai cung cấp dependency cho class.
Liên hệ với provider, module, lifecycle và khả năng test trong NestJS.
Dùng ví dụ nhỏ controller-service để câu trả lời cụ thể.
commonMistakes: Xem DI chỉ là import class từ file khác.
Không giải thích vì sao IoC container giúp mock hoặc thay implementation dễ hơn.
Bỏ qua việc provider vẫn phải được đăng ký trong module context.

ja.title: NestJS における Dependency Injection の目的
ja.displayQuestion: Dependency Injection とは何で、なぜ NestJS はそれを使うのですか。
ja.displayIntent: DI を単なる decorator ではなく、container が object wiring を管理する仕組みとして理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: dependency を誰が作り、誰が class に渡すのかという観点で定義する。
NestJS の provider、module、lifecycle、testing と関連づける。
controller と service の小さな例で具体化する。
commonMistakes: DI を class import と同じものとして扱う。
IoC container が mock や implementation 交換を容易にする理由を説明しない。
provider は module context に登録が必要である点を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for NestJS provider and dependency injection concepts.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for module registration and provider visibility.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands what @Injectable() marks, why metadata matters, and which NestJS classes are normally registered as injectable providers.
primaryQuestion: What is the @Injectable() decorator, and which classes require it?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Injectable() marks a class as a provider candidate that can be managed by the NestJS DI container.
Mentions that services, repositories, adapters, guards, interceptors, pipes, filters, and strategies commonly use @Injectable() when they are provider classes.
Distinguishes @Injectable() from @Controller() and @Module(), which have their own decorators and runtime roles.
Understands that registering the class in a module providers array is still needed unless it is provided through another mechanism.
Mentions that decorators and emitted metadata help Nest infer constructor dependencies, while custom tokens may still need @Inject().
redFlags: Says @Injectable() alone globally registers the class everywhere.
Thinks controllers should normally be decorated with @Injectable() instead of @Controller().
Cannot distinguish a class being decorated from a provider being visible in a module.
Assumes TypeScript interfaces can be injected directly without a runtime token.

scoringHints.description: Explains decorator metadata, provider role, common provider class types, module registration, and the distinction from controllers/modules.
Understands @Injectable() for services but misses metadata or module registration nuance.
Recognizes the decorator by name but cannot say what it changes in NestJS DI behavior.
Answer does not demonstrate practical knowledge of NestJS provider classes.

followUps.question: If a service has @Injectable() but is not listed in any module providers array, can another class inject it? Why?
followUps.purpose: Check module registration and visibility reasoning.

followUps.question: Why do controllers use @Controller() instead of only @Injectable()?
followUps.purpose: Verify that the candidate distinguishes NestJS class roles.

en.title: @Injectable provider metadata
en.displayQuestion: What is the @Injectable() decorator, and which classes require it?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate knows how provider classes become managed by NestJS DI.
guidance: Explain the relationship between @Injectable(), provider registration, and constructor metadata.
Name common injectable class types such as services, guards, pipes, interceptors, filters, and repositories.
Contrast provider classes with controllers and modules.
commonMistakes: Thinking @Injectable() automatically exports the class to the whole app.
Forgetting the providers array or equivalent registration.
Treating TypeScript interfaces as runtime injection tokens.

vi.title: Metadata provider với @Injectable
vi.displayQuestion: @Injectable() decorator là gì, và những class nào cần nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết cách class provider được NestJS DI quản lý hay không.
guidance: Giải thích quan hệ giữa @Injectable(), đăng ký provider và constructor metadata.
Kể tên các class thường injectable như service, guard, pipe, interceptor, filter và repository.
Phân biệt provider class với controller và module.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ rằng @Injectable() tự export class ra toàn app.
Quên providers array hoặc cơ chế đăng ký tương đương.
Xem TypeScript interface như runtime injection token.

ja.title: @Injectable による provider metadata
ja.displayQuestion: @Injectable() decorator とは何で、どの class に必要ですか。
ja.displayIntent: provider class が NestJS DI によって管理される仕組みを理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: @Injectable()、provider registration、constructor metadata の関係を説明する。
service、guard、pipe、interceptor、filter、repository など一般的な injectable class を挙げる。
provider class と controller/module の違いを説明する。
commonMistakes: @Injectable() が class を自動的に全 app へ export すると考える。
providers array などの登録を忘れる。
TypeScript interface を runtime injection token として扱う。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Injectable() and provider registration behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for token and provider registration nuance.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can compare constructor injection and property injection in NestJS and choose constructor injection for explicit required dependencies.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between constructor injection and property injection?
expectedSignals: Explains constructor injection as declaring dependencies in the constructor so the class cannot be instantiated correctly without them.
Explains property injection as assigning dependencies to decorated properties after class construction.
States that constructor injection is the usual NestJS choice because dependencies are explicit, required, easier to test, and compatible with readonly fields.
Mentions property injection may be used for optional or framework-driven cases, but it hides dependencies and can leave properties undefined during construction.
Connects the choice to maintainability: constructor parameters make dependencies visible in tests and code review.
redFlags: Says property injection is always cleaner because it avoids long constructors.
Does not recognize that property-injected values are not available inside the constructor body.
Cannot explain why explicit required dependencies are better expressed in the constructor.
Confuses property injection with TypeScript class property initialization.

scoringHints.description: Compares timing, explicitness, required versus optional dependencies, testability, and NestJS convention with a concrete example.
Prefers constructor injection and explains the main reason but misses timing or optional-dependency nuance.
Can name both styles but cannot choose between them in real NestJS code.
Answer does not demonstrate practical DI style knowledge.

followUps.question: When would property injection be risky in a service that uses a dependency during construction?
followUps.purpose: Check lifecycle and initialization-order understanding.

followUps.question: How would your unit test reveal the dependencies of a class that uses constructor injection?
followUps.purpose: Connect injection style to test ergonomics.

en.title: Constructor versus property injection
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between constructor injection and property injection?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can choose an injection style based on explicitness, lifecycle timing, and testability.
guidance: Compare when dependencies become available.
Explain why constructor injection is preferred for required dependencies.
Mention the trade-off of hiding dependencies with property injection.
commonMistakes: Using property injection only to shorten constructor signatures.
Assuming property-injected dependencies are available inside the constructor.
Not considering how tests discover and supply dependencies.

vi.title: Constructor injection và property injection
vi.displayQuestion: Constructor injection và property injection khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có chọn được style injection dựa trên độ rõ ràng, thời điểm lifecycle và khả năng test hay không.
guidance: So sánh thời điểm dependency sẵn sàng để dùng.
Giải thích vì sao constructor injection phù hợp hơn cho dependency bắt buộc.
Nêu trade-off của việc giấu dependency bằng property injection.
commonMistakes: Dùng property injection chỉ để constructor ngắn hơn.
Tưởng dependency inject vào property có thể dùng ngay trong constructor.
Không cân nhắc test sẽ phát hiện và cung cấp dependency như thế nào.

ja.title: constructor injection と property injection
ja.displayQuestion: constructor injection と property injection の違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: 明示性、lifecycle timing、testability に基づいて injection style を選べるかを確認する。
guidance: dependency が利用可能になるタイミングを比較する。
必須 dependency には constructor injection が好まれる理由を説明する。
property injection が dependency を隠しやすい trade-off に触れる。
commonMistakes: constructor を短くする目的だけで property injection を使う。
property-injected dependency が constructor 内で使えると考える。
test が dependency をどう把握し供給するかを考慮しない。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for constructor-based dependency injection patterns.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for explicit token injection and provider binding patterns.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands DEFAULT, REQUEST, and TRANSIENT provider scopes and can justify REQUEST scope without overusing it.
primaryQuestion: What are Provider scopes in NestJS (DEFAULT, REQUEST, TRANSIENT)? When would you use REQUEST scope?
expectedSignals: Defines DEFAULT scope as singleton-like within the application/module context and reused across requests.
Defines REQUEST scope as creating a provider instance per incoming request context.
Defines TRANSIENT scope as creating a new provider instance for each injection consumer.
Mentions that REQUEST scope has performance and lifecycle implications and can propagate through dependency chains.
Uses REQUEST scope for request-specific state such as tenant context, current user context, correlation metadata, or per-request transaction context when passing parameters is not clean.
Warns against storing mutable request data in DEFAULT singleton providers.
redFlags: Says REQUEST scope should be used for every service that handles HTTP requests.
Stores current user or tenant state in a singleton provider field.
Cannot explain the difference between REQUEST and TRANSIENT.
Ignores the overhead and dependency-chain impact of request-scoped providers.

scoringHints.description: Accurately defines all three scopes, explains lifecycle/performance trade-offs, and gives a justified request-specific use case.
Defines the scopes correctly but gives a shallow REQUEST scope use case or misses scope propagation.
Knows singleton and request words but cannot reason about mutable state or provider lifetime.
Answer does not show practical knowledge of NestJS provider scopes.

followUps.question: Why can REQUEST scope hurt performance or make a dependency graph harder to reason about?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands scope cost and propagation.

followUps.question: What bug could happen if you store currentUser on a singleton service field?
followUps.purpose: Verify awareness of cross-request state leaks.

en.title: Provider scopes and REQUEST scope
en.displayQuestion: What are Provider scopes in NestJS (DEFAULT, REQUEST, TRANSIENT)? When would you use REQUEST scope?
en.displayIntent: Check provider lifecycle reasoning and whether the candidate avoids request-state bugs.
guidance: Define each scope by instance lifetime.
Explain REQUEST scope with a request-specific state example.
Discuss performance and dependency-chain trade-offs.
commonMistakes: Using REQUEST scope as a default for HTTP services.
Keeping per-request mutable state in singleton providers.
Confusing transient per-consumer instances with per-request instances.

vi.title: Provider scope và REQUEST scope
vi.displayQuestion: Provider scopes trong NestJS (DEFAULT, REQUEST, TRANSIENT) là gì? Khi nào bạn dùng REQUEST scope?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra tư duy lifecycle của provider và khả năng tránh lỗi request-state.
guidance: Định nghĩa từng scope theo vòng đời instance.
Giải thích REQUEST scope bằng ví dụ state riêng theo request.
Nêu trade-off về performance và dependency chain.
commonMistakes: Dùng REQUEST scope mặc định cho mọi HTTP service.
Giữ mutable state theo request trong singleton provider.
Nhầm transient instance theo consumer với request instance theo request.

ja.title: provider scope と REQUEST scope
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の Provider scope (DEFAULT, REQUEST, TRANSIENT) とは何ですか。いつ REQUEST scope を使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: provider lifecycle の理解と request-state bug を避ける判断力を確認する。
guidance: 各 scope を instance lifetime で定義する。
request 固有 state の例で REQUEST scope を説明する。
performance と dependency chain の trade-off を述べる。
commonMistakes: HTTP service には常に REQUEST scope を使うと考える。
request ごとの mutable state を singleton provider に保持する。
consumer ごとの transient instance と request ごとの instance を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Injection scopes
sourceReferences.note: Used for DEFAULT, REQUEST, and TRANSIENT provider lifetimes.

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish singleton provider reuse from transient provider instantiation and reason about state, memory, and dependency behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a singleton and a transient provider?
expectedSignals: Explains that a singleton provider has one shared instance reused by consumers within the application/module context.
Explains that a transient provider creates a separate instance for each injection consumer.
Mentions singleton is the NestJS default and is appropriate for stateless services, database clients, repositories, and shared adapters.
Mentions transient can be useful for lightweight stateful helpers, per-consumer builders, contextual loggers, or classes that should not share internal mutable fields.
Identifies the risk of mutable shared state in singletons and the overhead of creating many transient instances.
redFlags: Says transient means one instance per HTTP request.
Assumes singleton providers are unsafe by default because requests are concurrent.
Uses transient to hide state bugs instead of designing stateless services.
Cannot explain why database connections or expensive clients usually should not be transient.

scoringHints.description: Clearly compares instance lifetime, default behavior, state safety, memory/performance implications, and realistic use cases.
Defines singleton and transient correctly but gives limited examples or misses overhead concerns.
Can repeat definitions but confuses transient with request scope or cannot apply the distinction.
Answer is too vague to confirm provider lifetime understanding.

followUps.question: Would you make a database client transient? Why or why not?
followUps.purpose: Check performance and resource-lifecycle reasoning.

followUps.question: If a singleton service has a mutable array field, what concurrency or data-leak issue could appear?
followUps.purpose: Verify awareness of shared mutable state risk.

en.title: Singleton versus transient providers
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a singleton and a transient provider?
en.displayIntent: Assess whether the candidate understands provider instance lifetime and state-sharing implications.
guidance: State how many instances are created and who shares them.
Name good candidates for singleton providers.
Describe a valid transient use case and its cost.
commonMistakes: Confusing transient scope with request scope.
Putting request-specific mutable data into singleton fields.
Making expensive clients transient without a resource reason.

vi.title: Singleton provider và transient provider
vi.displayQuestion: Singleton provider và transient provider khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có hiểu vòng đời instance của provider và hệ quả khi chia sẻ state hay không.
guidance: Nêu số lượng instance được tạo và ai dùng chung instance đó.
Kể các trường hợp phù hợp với singleton provider.
Mô tả một use case transient hợp lý và chi phí của nó.
commonMistakes: Nhầm transient scope với request scope.
Đưa mutable data riêng theo request vào field của singleton.
Biến expensive client thành transient mà không có lý do về resource.

ja.title: singleton provider と transient provider
ja.displayQuestion: singleton provider と transient provider の違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: provider instance lifetime と state sharing の影響を理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: instance がいくつ作られ、誰が共有するのかを述べる。
singleton provider に適した例を挙げる。
transient の妥当な use case と cost を説明する。
commonMistakes: transient scope と request scope を混同する。
request 固有の mutable data を singleton field に入れる。
高コストな client を理由なく transient にする。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Injection scopes
sourceReferences.note: Used for singleton/default and transient provider lifetime distinctions.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can implement custom providers with useClass, useValue, and useFactory and choose the right provider style for swapping implementations, constants, and dynamic configuration.
primaryQuestion: How do you create a custom provider using useClass, useValue, and useFactory?
expectedSignals: Explains the common provider object shape with provide as the injection token and one of useClass, useValue, or useFactory as the binding.
Describes useClass for choosing or swapping an implementation class behind a token, such as different mailer implementations.
Describes useValue for static values, configuration objects, constants, external client instances, or test mocks.
Describes useFactory for dynamic creation using injected dependencies, environment config, async setup, or conditional logic.
Mentions that custom tokens can be strings, symbols, or class references and that consumers use @Inject(token) when TypeScript type inference is not enough.
Notes that the provider must be registered in the module and exported if another module needs to inject it.
redFlags: Cannot name the provide token or explain how consumers request the provider.
Uses useFactory without mentioning its inject array for dependencies.
Uses useValue for mutable shared objects without considering shared state.
Assumes a TypeScript interface can be used directly as an injection token at runtime.

scoringHints.description: Explains all three provider styles, token selection, inject array, module export, and testing/configuration use cases.
Understands the three styles but misses token/runtime interface nuance or module export detail.
Has used custom providers but cannot explain when each style should be chosen.
Answer does not demonstrate working knowledge of NestJS custom provider syntax.

followUps.question: Can you sketch a provider object for CONFIG using useValue and a provider object for Mailer using useClass?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete provider syntax.

followUps.question: How does a useFactory provider receive ConfigService or another dependency?
followUps.purpose: Verify inject-array mechanics.

en.title: Custom providers with useClass, useValue, useFactory
en.displayQuestion: How do you create a custom provider using useClass, useValue, and useFactory?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can bind provider tokens to classes, values, and factories in real NestJS modules.
guidance: Describe the provider object shape: provide plus a binding strategy.
Explain when to choose useClass, useValue, or useFactory.
Mention custom tokens, @Inject(), inject arrays, and exports.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the injection token.
Using a TypeScript interface as if it exists at runtime.
Not exporting the custom provider for other modules.

vi.title: Custom provider với useClass, useValue, useFactory
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn tạo custom provider bằng useClass, useValue và useFactory như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết bind provider token với class, value và factory trong module NestJS thực tế hay không.
guidance: Mô tả shape của provider object: provide cộng với một chiến lược binding.
Giải thích khi nào chọn useClass, useValue hoặc useFactory.
Nhắc tới custom token, @Inject(), inject array và exports.
commonMistakes: Quên injection token.
Dùng TypeScript interface như thể nó tồn tại ở runtime.
Không export custom provider cho module khác.

ja.title: useClass, useValue, useFactory による custom provider
ja.displayQuestion: useClass、useValue、useFactory を使って custom provider をどう作りますか。
ja.displayIntent: 実際の NestJS module で provider token を class、value、factory に bind できるかを確認する。
guidance: provider object の形、つまり provide と binding strategy を説明する。
useClass、useValue、useFactory の使い分けを説明する。
custom token、@Inject()、inject array、exports に触れる。
commonMistakes: injection token を忘れる。
TypeScript interface が runtime に存在すると考える。
他 module で使う custom provider を export しない。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for useClass, useValue, useFactory, and injection token behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for module exports and provider visibility.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands circular dependencies in providers/modules and prioritizes refactoring before using forwardRef as a workaround.
primaryQuestion: What is a circular dependency, and how do you resolve it in NestJS?
expectedSignals: Defines a circular dependency as two or more providers or modules depending on each other directly or indirectly.
Explains why cycles make initialization order and ownership unclear and can produce dependency resolution errors.
Prioritizes design fixes such as extracting shared logic to a third service/module, inverting the dependency, using events, or splitting responsibilities.
Mentions forwardRef() as a NestJS mechanism for unavoidable provider or module cycles, but not the first design choice.
Notes that module-level cycles require forwardRef in imports on both sides when both modules import each other.
Mentions alternatives such as ModuleRef or lazy lookup only for edge cases where refactoring is not practical.
redFlags: Uses forwardRef() as the default solution without analyzing ownership.
Cannot distinguish a TypeScript import cycle from a NestJS DI provider/module cycle.
Adds both services to every module to make the error disappear.
Does not understand that circular dependency often signals poor boundaries or mixed responsibilities.

scoringHints.description: Defines provider and module cycles, explains root causes, proposes refactoring options, and uses forwardRef only as a controlled workaround.
Explains cycles and forwardRef but misses design-level fixes or module/provider distinction.
Recognizes the error but only knows to paste forwardRef without reasoning.
Answer does not show practical debugging or design understanding of DI cycles.

followUps.question: If AuthService and UsersService depend on each other, what shared responsibility might you extract before using forwardRef?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate can break a real cycle by design.

followUps.question: What problem remains if you fix every cycle by adding forwardRef everywhere?
followUps.purpose: Verify awareness that forwardRef can hide poor boundaries.

en.title: Circular dependencies in NestJS DI
en.displayQuestion: What is a circular dependency, and how do you resolve it in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can diagnose dependency cycles and fix boundaries instead of only applying forwardRef.
guidance: Define cycles for both providers and modules.
Prioritize refactoring, extraction, events, or dependency inversion.
Explain when forwardRef or lazy lookup is acceptable.
commonMistakes: Treating forwardRef as the default architecture pattern.
Confusing TypeScript import cycles with DI cycles.
Registering providers in unrelated modules to silence the error.

vi.title: Circular dependency trong NestJS DI
vi.displayQuestion: Circular dependency là gì, và bạn xử lý nó trong NestJS như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có chẩn đoán được dependency cycle và sửa boundary thay vì chỉ thêm forwardRef hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa cycle cho cả provider và module.
Ưu tiên refactor, extract shared service, event hoặc dependency inversion.
Giải thích khi nào forwardRef hoặc lazy lookup là chấp nhận được.
commonMistakes: Xem forwardRef là pattern kiến trúc mặc định.
Nhầm TypeScript import cycle với DI cycle.
Đăng ký provider vào module không liên quan chỉ để hết lỗi.

ja.title: NestJS DI の circular dependency
ja.displayQuestion: circular dependency とは何で、NestJS ではどう解決しますか。
ja.displayIntent: dependency cycle を診断し、forwardRef だけでなく境界を修正できるかを確認する。
guidance: provider と module の両方の cycle を定義する。
refactor、shared service の抽出、event、dependency inversion を優先する。
forwardRef や lazy lookup が許容される場面を説明する。
commonMistakes: forwardRef を標準の architecture pattern として扱う。
TypeScript import cycle と DI cycle を混同する。
error を消すために無関係な module へ provider を登録する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Circular dependency
sourceReferences.note: Used for forwardRef and circular dependency handling.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for module import/export boundary reasoning.

intent: Verify whether the candidate knows what forwardRef() does, where it is applied, and why it should be used sparingly.
primaryQuestion: What is forwardRef(), and when is it needed?
expectedSignals: Explains forwardRef() as a wrapper that lets Nest resolve a reference lazily when two providers or modules refer to each other.
Mentions module usage such as imports: [forwardRef(() => UsersModule)] when two modules import each other.
Mentions provider usage such as @Inject(forwardRef(() => UsersService)) when two providers inject each other.
States that both sides of a provider or module cycle may need the appropriate forwardRef wiring.
Frames forwardRef as a workaround for unavoidable cycles after considering refactoring.
Warns that it can make architecture harder to reason about and should not replace clear ownership boundaries.
redFlags: Says forwardRef fixes any injection error.
Uses forwardRef when the real problem is a missing provider, missing export, or wrong module import.
Cannot state whether the cycle is between modules or providers.
Does not know where forwardRef appears in code.

scoringHints.description: Explains lazy reference resolution, provider and module syntax, bidirectional wiring, and the architectural trade-off.
Knows forwardRef solves cycles but misses either provider syntax, module syntax, or design caution.
Recognizes the name but cannot apply it correctly to a provider or module cycle.
Answer does not distinguish circular dependencies from other DI errors.

followUps.question: How would you tell whether the fix belongs in module imports or in a constructor @Inject() parameter?
followUps.purpose: Check provider-versus-module cycle diagnosis.

followUps.question: If the provider simply is not exported from its module, would forwardRef help?
followUps.purpose: Verify that the candidate does not overapply forwardRef.

en.title: forwardRef for lazy DI references
en.displayQuestion: What is forwardRef(), and when is it needed?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can use forwardRef correctly for unavoidable circular provider or module references.
guidance: Explain lazy resolution of circular references.
Show where forwardRef is used for modules and providers.
State why missing exports or missing providers are different problems.
commonMistakes: Using forwardRef for every DI error.
Not knowing whether the cycle is provider-level or module-level.
Avoiding a simpler boundary refactor.

vi.title: forwardRef cho DI reference lazy
vi.displayQuestion: forwardRef() là gì, và khi nào cần dùng nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có dùng forwardRef đúng cho circular reference không tránh được giữa provider hoặc module hay không.
guidance: Giải thích cơ chế lazy resolution cho circular reference.
Nêu forwardRef được dùng ở đâu với module và provider.
Chỉ rõ missing export hoặc missing provider là lỗi khác.
commonMistakes: Dùng forwardRef cho mọi lỗi DI.
Không biết cycle nằm ở provider-level hay module-level.
Né refactor boundary đơn giản hơn.

ja.title: lazy DI reference のための forwardRef
ja.displayQuestion: forwardRef() とは何で、いつ必要ですか。
ja.displayIntent: 避けられない provider/module の circular reference に forwardRef を正しく使えるかを確認する。
guidance: circular reference の lazy resolution を説明する。
module と provider で forwardRef をどこに使うかを示す。
missing export や missing provider は別問題であると述べる。
commonMistakes: すべての DI error に forwardRef を使う。
cycle が provider-level か module-level かを把握しない。
より単純な boundary refactor を避ける。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Circular dependency
sourceReferences.note: Used for forwardRef provider and module examples.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Common errors
sourceReferences.note: Used for distinguishing circular dependencies from missing provider/export errors.

intent: Check whether the candidate knows why @Inject() is needed for explicit provider tokens, non-class values, interfaces, and special resolution cases.
primaryQuestion: What is the @Inject() decorator used for?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Inject() explicitly tells Nest which provider token to resolve for a constructor parameter or property.
Mentions @Inject() is needed for custom tokens such as strings, symbols, constants, or abstract tokens that TypeScript cannot infer at runtime.
Explains that TypeScript interfaces are erased at runtime, so a concrete injection token is required.
Mentions use cases such as injecting configuration values, external clients, aliases, and custom provider implementations.
Connects @Inject() with forwardRef() in circular provider cases when lazy token resolution is required.
redFlags: Says @Inject() is required for every constructor-injected service.
Assumes interfaces can be injected without a runtime token.
Cannot explain the difference between a TypeScript type annotation and a NestJS provider token.
Uses string tokens casually without considering consistency or symbol/constant tokens.

scoringHints.description: Explains explicit token resolution, runtime type erasure, custom provider tokens, and practical examples.
Understands @Inject() for custom tokens but misses interface erasure or forwardRef use.
Knows the decorator name but cannot say when Nest cannot infer a dependency automatically.
Answer is too vague to validate provider-token understanding.

followUps.question: Why can Nest usually inject UsersService without @Inject(), but not inject an interface named CachePort?
followUps.purpose: Check metadata and interface-erasure understanding.

followUps.question: How would you inject a provider registered with provide: 'APP_CONFIG'?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete use of @Inject() with a non-class token.

en.title: @Inject and explicit provider tokens
en.displayQuestion: What is the @Inject() decorator used for?
en.displayIntent: Assess whether the candidate understands explicit token-based dependency resolution in NestJS.
guidance: Explain why class types can often be inferred but custom tokens cannot.
Mention string, symbol, constant, and abstract tokens.
Connect @Inject() to useValue, useFactory, useClass, and interface erasure.
commonMistakes: Adding @Inject() everywhere without knowing why.
Trying to inject a TypeScript interface directly.
Using magic string tokens inconsistently.

vi.title: @Inject và provider token tường minh
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có hiểu cơ chế resolve dependency bằng token tường minh trong NestJS hay không.
guidance: Giải thích vì sao class type thường suy luận được nhưng custom token thì không.
Nhắc tới string, symbol, constant và abstract token.
Liên hệ @Inject() với useValue, useFactory, useClass và việc interface bị xóa ở runtime.
commonMistakes: Thêm @Inject() ở mọi nơi mà không biết lý do.
Cố inject trực tiếp TypeScript interface.
Dùng magic string token không nhất quán.

ja.title: @Inject と明示的な provider token
ja.displayQuestion: @Inject() decorator は何に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS の token-based dependency resolution を理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: class type は推論できることが多いが custom token は推論できない理由を説明する。
string、symbol、constant、abstract token に触れる。
@Inject() を useValue、useFactory、useClass、interface erasure と関連づける。
commonMistakes: 理由なくあらゆる場所に @Inject() を追加する。
TypeScript interface を直接 inject しようとする。
magic string token を一貫性なく使う。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for explicit provider tokens and @Inject() usage.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for constructor injection and token inference behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands how NestJS DI differs from manual object construction and the bugs caused by bypassing the container.
primaryQuestion: How does NestJS DI differ from manually calling new ServiceName()?
expectedSignals: Explains that NestJS DI uses the IoC container to resolve constructor dependencies, provider tokens, scopes, lifecycle hooks, and module visibility.
Explains that new ServiceName() bypasses the container and therefore bypasses nested dependency injection.
Mentions manual construction can create duplicate instances, ignore singleton/request/transient scope, and miss configuration or mocked implementations.
Connects DI to testing because tests can override providers without changing the class under test.
Mentions that manual new may be acceptable for plain value objects or local helper classes that are not Nest providers and have no DI needs.
redFlags: Says manual new is equivalent if the constructor arguments are the same.
Manually creates services inside controllers or other providers as a normal pattern.
Does not recognize lifecycle, scope, mocking, or nested dependency implications.
Cannot explain why a service with its own injected dependencies fails when constructed manually.

scoringHints.description: Contrasts container-managed construction with manual new across dependency graph, scopes, lifecycle, module boundaries, and tests.
Explains testability and nested dependency injection but misses scope or lifecycle detail.
Knows DI avoids new but cannot explain practical consequences.
Answer does not demonstrate understanding of NestJS container behavior.

followUps.question: What happens if UsersService manually constructs EmailService, but EmailService expects ConfigService injected by Nest?
followUps.purpose: Check nested dependency and container-bypass reasoning.

followUps.question: When is it still reasonable to use new for an object in a NestJS application?
followUps.purpose: Verify nuanced distinction between providers and plain domain/value objects.

en.title: DI container versus manual new
en.displayQuestion: How does NestJS DI differ from manually calling new ServiceName()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands the consequences of bypassing the NestJS IoC container.
guidance: Compare dependency graph resolution and lifecycle behavior.
Mention provider scopes, module boundaries, and test overrides.
Distinguish Nest providers from plain domain or value objects.
commonMistakes: Manually instantiating providers inside controllers.
Forgetting nested dependencies and provider scopes.
Treating every class in the app as a Nest provider.

vi.title: DI container so với gọi new thủ công
vi.displayQuestion: NestJS DI khác gì so với việc gọi thủ công new ServiceName()?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu hệ quả khi bypass NestJS IoC container hay không.
guidance: So sánh cách resolve dependency graph và hành vi lifecycle.
Nhắc tới provider scope, module boundary và test override.
Phân biệt Nest provider với domain object hoặc value object thuần.
commonMistakes: Tự new provider bên trong controller.
Quên nested dependency và provider scope.
Xem mọi class trong app đều là Nest provider.

ja.title: DI container と手動 new の違い
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS DI は手動で new ServiceName() を呼ぶ場合と何が違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS IoC container を bypass する影響を理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: dependency graph resolution と lifecycle behavior を比較する。
provider scope、module boundary、test override に触れる。
Nest provider と plain domain/value object を区別する。
commonMistakes: controller 内で provider を手動で new する。
nested dependency と provider scope を忘れる。
app 内の全 class を Nest provider として扱う。

sourceReferences.note: Used for container-managed provider construction.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Testing
sourceReferences.note: Used for provider overriding and test module behavior.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands optional dependencies, when they are appropriate, and how @Optional() changes dependency resolution behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is an optional dependency, and how do you mark one with @Optional()?
expectedSignals: Defines an optional dependency as one that the class can operate without, often because a feature, adapter, logger, or integration is optional.
Explains that @Optional() is applied to a constructor parameter or injected property so Nest does not fail if the token is missing.
Mentions that optional dependencies often pair with @Inject(token), especially for custom tokens or pluggable integrations.
States that the code must handle undefined or absence explicitly instead of assuming the provider exists.
Warns against using @Optional() to hide missing module imports, missing exports, or incorrectly registered mandatory dependencies.
Gives an example such as an optional metrics client, optional cache adapter, optional feature flag service, or optional logger.
redFlags: Uses @Optional() to silence DI errors for required dependencies.
Does not handle the dependency being undefined.
Cannot name a real optional integration use case.
Confuses optional TypeScript constructor parameter syntax with NestJS optional provider resolution.

scoringHints.description: Explains @Optional() behavior, syntax, undefined handling, custom tokens, valid use cases, and misuse risks.
Understands optional dependencies but misses custom-token pairing or misuse risk.
Knows @Optional() exists but treats it as a generic fix for DI errors.
Answer does not demonstrate safe optional dependency handling.

followUps.question: If PaymentService cannot work without PaymentGateway, should you mark PaymentGateway as @Optional()? Why?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate distinguishes optional from required dependencies.

followUps.question: How should the service behave when an optional metrics client is not registered?
followUps.purpose: Verify explicit absence handling.

en.title: Optional dependencies with @Optional
en.displayQuestion: What is an optional dependency, and how do you mark one with @Optional()?
en.displayIntent: Assess whether the candidate can model optional integrations without hiding real DI errors.
guidance: Define what makes a dependency truly optional.
Explain @Optional() placement and undefined handling.
Warn against using it for mandatory dependencies.
commonMistakes: Using @Optional() to silence a missing provider error.
Forgetting null or undefined handling in the service.
Confusing TypeScript optional syntax with NestJS DI resolution.

vi.title: Optional dependency với @Optional
vi.displayQuestion: Optional dependency là gì, và bạn đánh dấu nó bằng @Optional() như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có mô hình hóa optional integration đúng cách mà không che lỗi DI thật hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa khi nào dependency thật sự optional.
Giải thích vị trí đặt @Optional() và cách xử lý undefined.
Cảnh báo không dùng nó cho dependency bắt buộc.
commonMistakes: Dùng @Optional() để che lỗi missing provider.
Quên xử lý null hoặc undefined trong service.
Nhầm optional syntax của TypeScript với cơ chế DI resolution của NestJS.

ja.title: @Optional による optional dependency
ja.displayQuestion: optional dependency とは何で、@Optional() でどう示しますか。
ja.displayIntent: 本当の DI error を隠さず、optional integration を正しく扱えるかを確認する。
guidance: dependency が本当に optional である条件を定義する。
@Optional() の配置と undefined handling を説明する。
必須 dependency に使うべきでない理由を述べる。
commonMistakes: missing provider error を隠すために @Optional() を使う。
service 側で null/undefined handling を忘れる。
TypeScript の optional syntax と NestJS DI resolution を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for optional provider dependency behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for optional custom-token dependencies.

intent: Check whether the candidate can inject non-class values through custom providers and understands token-based resolution for configuration, constants, and external instances.
primaryQuestion: How can you inject a plain value (not a class) as a provider?
expectedSignals: Explains registering a custom provider with a token and useValue, such as { provide: APP_CONFIG, useValue: configObject }.
Mentions that the consumer injects the value with @Inject(APP_CONFIG) because there is no class type for Nest to infer.
States that tokens should be stable constants or symbols rather than scattered magic strings.
Mentions plain values can include config objects, constants, feature flags, external SDK clients, or mocks.
Notes that useValue provides the same object reference to consumers, so mutable shared values should be treated carefully.
Mentions exporting the provider if other modules need to inject the plain value.
redFlags: Tries to inject a plain object by only adding it to a constructor type annotation.
Uses a TypeScript interface as the provider token.
Forgets @Inject() for a string or symbol token.
Does not consider mutability and shared reference behavior of useValue.

scoringHints.description: Explains token constants, useValue registration, @Inject consumption, module exports, and shared-reference caveats.
Understands useValue and @Inject but misses token hygiene or mutability/export nuance.
Can vaguely say useValue but cannot describe the module provider and consumer sides.
Answer does not show practical knowledge of injecting non-class providers.

followUps.question: What would the providers array and constructor parameter look like for an APP_CONFIG object?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete provider registration and injection syntax.

followUps.question: What risk appears if the injected useValue object is mutable and shared across services?
followUps.purpose: Check shared-reference and state-safety reasoning.

en.title: Injecting plain values as providers
en.displayQuestion: How can you inject a plain value (not a class) as a provider?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands useValue and custom tokens for non-class dependencies.
guidance: Describe the provider object with provide and useValue.
Explain why @Inject(token) is needed on the consumer side.
Mention token constants and shared mutable value risks.
commonMistakes: Trying to inject interfaces or plain types without a runtime token.
Using untracked magic strings as tokens.
Mutating a shared useValue object without intent.

vi.title: Inject plain value như provider
vi.displayQuestion: Làm sao inject một plain value, không phải class, như một provider?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu useValue và custom token cho dependency không phải class hay không.
guidance: Mô tả provider object với provide và useValue.
Giải thích vì sao phía consumer cần @Inject(token).
Nhắc tới token constant và rủi ro mutable value dùng chung.
commonMistakes: Cố inject interface hoặc plain type mà không có runtime token.
Dùng magic string token rải rác.
Mutate object useValue dùng chung mà không chủ đích.

ja.title: plain value を provider として inject する
ja.displayQuestion: class ではない plain value を provider としてどう inject しますか。
ja.displayIntent: 非 class dependency に対する useValue と custom token を理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: provide と useValue を持つ provider object を説明する。
consumer 側で @Inject(token) が必要な理由を説明する。
token constant と shared mutable value のリスクに触れる。
commonMistakes: runtime token なしで interface や plain type を inject しようとする。
管理されていない magic string token を使う。
意図なく共有 useValue object を mutate する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom providers
sourceReferences.note: Used for useValue and non-class injection tokens.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can mock dependencies in NestJS unit tests using Test.createTestingModule and provider overrides.
primaryQuestion: How do you mock a provider in unit tests with Test.createTestingModule()?
expectedSignals: Explains creating a TestingModule with the class under test and a mock provider for its dependency.
Shows the provider shape { provide: DependencyToken, useValue: mockObject } for replacing a dependency.
Mentions module.compile() and retrieving the class under test with module.get(ServiceUnderTest).
Mentions jest.fn() or equivalent mocks to assert calls and control return values.
Mentions overrideProvider(token).useValue(mock) as an option when importing a real module but replacing one provider.
Keeps unit tests focused by mocking external dependencies such as repositories, HTTP clients, queues, or other services.
redFlags: Instantiates the service manually while ignoring its NestJS provider dependencies.
Imports the full application module for a narrow unit test without controlling dependencies.
Uses real database, queue, or HTTP clients in a unit test mock scenario.
Cannot identify the correct provider token to override.

scoringHints.description: Describes TestingModule setup, useValue mocks, module.get, jest.fn assertions, overrideProvider, and unit-test isolation.
Can replace dependencies with useValue but misses overrideProvider or retrieval/assertion detail.
Has tested NestJS code but cannot explain provider mocking mechanics.
Answer does not demonstrate practical NestJS unit testing knowledge.

followUps.question: If UserService depends on UserRepository, what providers would you pass to Test.createTestingModule()?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete unit-test module setup.

followUps.question: When would you use overrideProvider instead of declaring a useValue mock directly?
followUps.purpose: Check imported-module testing patterns.

en.title: Mocking providers in NestJS unit tests
en.displayQuestion: How do you mock a provider in unit tests with Test.createTestingModule()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can isolate a NestJS service by replacing dependencies in a TestingModule.
guidance: Describe Test.createTestingModule with the class under test and mock providers.
Use provide plus useValue and retrieve the service with module.get().
Mention overrideProvider for imported modules.
commonMistakes: Using real infrastructure dependencies in unit tests.
Manually new-ing a provider with an incomplete dependency graph.
Mocking the wrong token.

vi.title: Mock provider trong unit test NestJS
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn mock một provider trong unit test bằng Test.createTestingModule() như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có isolate được NestJS service bằng cách thay dependency trong TestingModule hay không.
guidance: Mô tả Test.createTestingModule với class đang test và mock provider.
Dùng provide cộng useValue và lấy service bằng module.get().
Nhắc tới overrideProvider khi import module thật.
commonMistakes: Dùng dependency hạ tầng thật trong unit test.
Tự new provider với dependency graph thiếu.
Mock sai token.

ja.title: NestJS unit test で provider を mock する
ja.displayQuestion: Test.createTestingModule() を使って unit test で provider をどう mock しますか。
ja.displayIntent: TestingModule 内で dependency を差し替え、NestJS service を isolate できるかを確認する。
guidance: test 対象 class と mock provider を含む Test.createTestingModule を説明する。
provide と useValue を使い、module.get() で service を取得する。
real module を import する場合の overrideProvider に触れる。
commonMistakes: unit test で real infrastructure dependency を使う。
不完全な dependency graph で provider を手動 new する。
間違った token を mock する。

sourceReferences.note: Used for Test.createTestingModule and provider override patterns.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands the NestJS IoC container as the runtime system that stores modules/providers and resolves dependency graphs.
primaryQuestion: What is the NestJS IoC container?
expectedSignals: Defines IoC container as the NestJS runtime component that owns provider registration, dependency resolution, and instance lifecycle.
Explains that modules contribute providers, controllers, imports, and exports to the container.
Mentions that the container resolves provider tokens and uses constructor metadata or explicit @Inject() tokens.
Explains that the container applies provider scopes such as singleton/default, request, and transient.
Connects IoC to inversion of control: application classes declare dependencies while the container creates and wires them.
Mentions that DI errors usually come from the container being unable to resolve a token in the current module context.
redFlags: Defines IoC container as only a place where controllers are stored.
Cannot connect modules and providers to dependency resolution.
Thinks the container scans all files automatically regardless of module registration.
Does not understand token resolution or module context.

scoringHints.description: Explains the container across module graph, provider tokens, constructor metadata, scopes, lifecycle, and DI error context.
Understands the container creates and injects providers but misses module graph or scope detail.
Can repeat the IoC term but cannot explain what the container does at runtime.
Answer does not show NestJS runtime dependency-resolution understanding.

followUps.question: How does the container know which implementation to inject for a constructor parameter?
followUps.purpose: Check token and metadata reasoning.

followUps.question: What role do module imports and exports play in what the container can resolve?
followUps.purpose: Connect IoC container behavior to NestJS module visibility.

en.title: NestJS IoC container
en.displayQuestion: What is the NestJS IoC container?
en.displayIntent: Assess whether the candidate understands the runtime component that resolves provider dependency graphs.
guidance: Define the container by its responsibilities.
Connect modules, providers, tokens, scopes, and lifecycle.
Explain inversion of control in practical NestJS terms.
commonMistakes: Thinking Nest scans and injects every class automatically.
Ignoring module context and provider exports.
Not distinguishing IoC from plain imports.

vi.title: NestJS IoC container
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá ứng viên có hiểu runtime component resolve dependency graph của provider hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa container qua các trách nhiệm của nó.
Liên hệ module, provider, token, scope và lifecycle.
Giải thích inversion of control theo ngữ cảnh NestJS thực tế.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ Nest tự scan và inject mọi class.
Bỏ qua module context và provider exports.
Không phân biệt IoC với import thông thường.

ja.title: NestJS IoC container
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS IoC container とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: provider dependency graph を resolve する runtime component を理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: container の責務から定義する。
module、provider、token、scope、lifecycle と関連づける。
NestJS の文脈で inversion of control を説明する。
commonMistakes: Nest がすべての class を自動 scan して inject すると考える。
module context と provider exports を無視する。
IoC と通常の import を区別しない。

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can debug the common NestJS error caused by injecting a provider that is missing from the module context.
primaryQuestion: What happens if you try to inject a provider that is not registered in any Module?
expectedSignals: States that NestJS fails during application bootstrap or testing module compilation with a dependency resolution error.
Mentions the typical message pattern: Nest cannot resolve dependencies of a class and shows the unresolved constructor parameter index or token.
Explains likely fixes: add the provider to the owning module providers array, import a module that exports it, or use the correct token.
Distinguishes missing provider from missing export: a provider can exist in another module but still be invisible unless exported and imported.
Mentions that @Global() modules can make exports available broadly, but should be used intentionally.
Notes that manually adding a provider to unrelated modules can create duplicate instances or weaken module boundaries.
redFlags: Says NestJS silently injects undefined for missing required providers.
Uses @Optional() or forwardRef() without diagnosing registration, import, or export issues.
Adds the missing service to AppModule only without considering module ownership.
Cannot read the error message to identify which constructor parameter failed.

scoringHints.description: Explains bootstrap/compile failure, reads the error context, identifies provider/import/export/token fixes, and preserves module boundaries.
Knows to register or import the provider but misses export visibility or token mismatch details.
Recognizes a DI error but applies random fixes like forwardRef or @Optional.
Answer does not demonstrate practical NestJS DI debugging ability.

followUps.question: If AuthService injects UsersService and the error happens in AuthModule, what should UsersModule expose and what should AuthModule import?
followUps.purpose: Check module ownership, exports, and imports.

followUps.question: Why is adding UsersService to both UsersModule and AuthModule not always the right fix?
followUps.purpose: Verify duplicate-instance and boundary reasoning.

en.title: Unregistered provider DI error
en.displayQuestion: What happens if you try to inject a provider that is not registered in any Module?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can diagnose and fix a common NestJS dependency resolution error.
guidance: Describe the bootstrap or test compile failure.
Explain how to read the unresolved dependency token or parameter index.
Name the correct fixes: providers, imports, exports, and token correction.
commonMistakes: Assuming Nest injects undefined for missing required dependencies.
Using forwardRef or @Optional before checking registration.
Registering the same service in unrelated modules and creating duplicate instances.

vi.title: Lỗi DI khi provider chưa đăng ký
vi.displayQuestion: Điều gì xảy ra nếu bạn inject một provider chưa được đăng ký trong bất kỳ Module nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có chẩn đoán và sửa được lỗi dependency resolution phổ biến trong NestJS hay không.
guidance: Mô tả lỗi khi bootstrap app hoặc compile testing module.
Giải thích cách đọc token hoặc index constructor parameter chưa resolve được.
Nêu fix đúng: providers, imports, exports và sửa token.
commonMistakes: Tưởng Nest inject undefined cho dependency bắt buộc bị thiếu.
Dùng forwardRef hoặc @Optional trước khi kiểm tra registration.
Đăng ký cùng service vào module không liên quan và tạo duplicate instance.

ja.title: 未登録 provider による DI error
ja.displayQuestion: どの Module にも登録されていない provider を inject しようとすると何が起きますか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS の一般的な dependency resolution error を診断し修正できるかを確認する。
guidance: app bootstrap または testing module compile の失敗を説明する。
resolve できない token または constructor parameter index の読み方を説明する。
正しい修正として providers、imports、exports、token correction を挙げる。
commonMistakes: 必須 dependency が欠けると Nest が undefined を inject すると考える。
registration を確認する前に forwardRef や @Optional を使う。
無関係な module に同じ service を登録し duplicate instance を作る。
